sourceMovie.title: Hancock
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto Hancock (2008) as a fantasy theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The fantasy and action blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Thor
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The fantasy appeal of Hancock — that tense edge, the action — translates cleanly into Thor (2011). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Man's Chest
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Man's Chest (2006) clearly understood what makes fantasy work — the same quality that keeps Hancock rewatchable. Expect the same tense rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: Pirates of the Caribbean: At World's End
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Pirates of the Caribbean: At World's End (2007) is the kind of film Hancock fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The fantasy overlap is real, but it's the tense emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness shares Hancock's gift for fantasy — the 2022 film delivers action without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Shazam!
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Hancock compelling — the fantasy, the action — shows up in Shazam! (2019) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that funny storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Castle in the Sky
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The fantasy energy of Hancock is alive in Castle in the Sky (1986), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a uplifting payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: Guardians of the Galaxy
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Guardians of the Galaxy (2014) operates in the same action as Hancock, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The tense payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Top Gun
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Top Gun (1986) belongs to the same conversation as Hancock — both anchor their action in drama emotion that doesn't let go. The bittersweet undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: The Age of Adaline
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Hancock's fantasy stayed with you, The Age of Adaline (2015) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The romance through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Peter Pan
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Hancock excels in fantasy, Peter Pan (1953) doubles down on animation to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

faq.question: What should I watch after Hancock?
faq.answer: Try Thor, Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Man's Chest, Pirates of the Caribbean: At World's End—each shares DNA with Hancock while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Hancock?
faq.answer: Hancock (2008) is best described as Fantasy, Action. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Hancock worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 6.4/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this fantasy lane.
